english: Grilled Chilean Sea Bass Teriyaki Set

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for teriyaki grilled mero set meal; mapped to broader 'fish dish (food)' as the closest defensible ancestor.
ontology.foodex2_description: Fish and fish products — grilled fish
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC (grilled fish) used as the closest defensible category for this teriyaki-glazed grilled sea bass set; no specific mero or teriyaki set code exists in FoodEx2.

main_ingredients: teriyaki sauce (soy sauce, mirin, sake, sugar)
miso soup (miso paste, dashi, tofu, wakame seaweed)
allergens: gluten (wheat in soy sauce and mirin)

serving.description: 1 set meal (grilled fish, rice, miso soup, side salad)
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard set meal portion of approximately 550 g, comprising a grilled mero fillet with teriyaki glaze, a bowl of steamed white rice, miso soup, and a side salad with sesame dressing.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — SR Legacy: Fish, sea bass, mixed species, cooked, dry heat; Rice, white, short-grain, cooked; Sauce, teriyaki, ready-to-serve; Miso soup, prepared
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA entry exists for a complete teriyaki grilled mero set meal. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted component averaging: grilled sea bass (~30% by weight), steamed white rice (~35%), miso soup (~25%), and side salad with sesame dressing (~10%). Chilean sea bass (Patagonian toothfish) is approximated using USDA SR Legacy 'Fish, sea bass, mixed species, cooked, dry heat' (FDC ID 175159) as the closest approved-source equivalent; mero is not separately listed in USDA FoodData Central. Teriyaki sauce contribution estimated from USDA SR Legacy 'Sauce, teriyaki, ready-to-serve' (FDC ID 172237). Input per_100g values (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.7) are retained as the primary numeric basis and cross-checked against USDA component estimates; values are consistent within expected range for this dish type.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; and University of Sydney GI Database — White rice, boiled (analogous estimate for rice-dominant mixed meal)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal (peer-reviewed)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No GI value exists in the University of Sydney GI Database for a teriyaki grilled mero set meal as a composite dish. GI estimated at 64 (medium) based on the dominant carbohydrate component: steamed white rice (GI ~64–72 per University of Sydney GI Database; conservative midpoint 64 used). The fish, miso soup, and salad components contribute negligible carbohydrates and are expected to modestly lower the effective GI of the composite meal through protein and fat co-ingestion. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (64 × 59.95) / 100 ≈ 38.4, where carbohydrate per serving = 10.9 g/100g × 550 g = 59.95 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium load from teriyaki sauce, miso paste, soy sauce, and sesame dressing combined
hypertension.guidance: At 327 mg sodium per 100 g (approximately 1,800 mg per full 550 g serving), this set meal substantially exceeds the recommended single-meal sodium ceiling of 800 mg for hypertensive individuals; patients should request reduced-sodium soy sauce and miso, apply teriyaki glaze sparingly, and take sesame dressing on the side.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycaemic load from white rice base (GL ≈ 38 per serving) with low dietary fibre (0.7 g/100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: The white rice component drives a glycaemic load of approximately 38 per serving — well above the low-GL threshold of 10 — making this meal unsuitable for blood glucose management without substitution; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should request brown rice or cauliflower rice, limit teriyaki sauce (contains mirin and sugar), and note that the mero, tofu, and seaweed components are diabetes-compatible.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Beneficial omega-3 fatty acids from Chilean sea bass offset by high sodium and moderate saturated fat
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Chilean sea bass provides EPA and DHA omega-3 fatty acids that support cardiovascular health, and saturated fat is moderate at 1.5 g/100 g; however, the high sodium content (~1,800 mg per serving) is a significant cardiovascular risk factor, and patients with CVD should request low-sodium preparation and limit teriyaki glaze to reduce both sodium and added sugar intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory omega-3 fatty acids from mero; pro-inflammatory risk from high sodium and added sugar in teriyaki sauce
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Chilean sea bass provides meaningful EPA/DHA omega-3 content that supports joint inflammation reduction; however, the high sodium from miso and soy sauce may worsen RA-related tissue swelling, and the sugar in teriyaki sauce is pro-inflammatory — patients should request reduced-sodium versions and light teriyaki application, and monitor for nightshade sensitivity if tomato is included in the salad.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Teriyaki Grilled Mero Set Meal is a Japanese restaurant set meal (teishoku) featuring a grilled fillet of mero (Chilean sea bass, Patagonian toothfish) coated in a sweet-savoury teriyaki glaze made from soy sauce, mirin, sake, and sugar. The fish is grilled until the glaze caramelises, producing a lacquered, slightly charred exterior with a moist, buttery, and mildly sweet interior. The set is typically served with a bowl of steamed Japanese short-grain white rice, a bowl of miso soup containing tofu, wakame seaweed, and dashi broth, and a side salad dressed with sesame dressing. Chilean sea bass is prized for its high fat content and rich, velvety texture, making it one of the most luxurious fish used in Japanese grilled fish preparations. Nutritionally, the set meal provides approximately 136 kcal per 100 g, with 8.2 g protein, 6.4 g fat, and 10.9 g carbohydrate per 100 g. A standard 550 g serving delivers roughly 750 kcal. Sodium is elevated at 327 mg/100 g (~1,800 mg per serving) due to the cumulative contribution of teriyaki sauce, miso paste, and sesame dressing. The glycaemic load per serving is high (~38), driven by the white rice component. The dish is clinically unsuitable for hypertension and Type 2 Diabetes without modification, but the omega-3 fatty acids from mero offer cardiovascular and anti-inflammatory benefits. This dish is commonly found in Japanese casual dining restaurants in Singapore and is a popular lunch or dinner set meal option.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a teriyaki grilled fish set?
Is mero fish good for diabetes?
Is teriyaki set meal high in sodium?
Can I eat teriyaki fish set if I have high blood pressure?
What is Chilean sea bass in Japanese cuisine?
Is mero set meal suitable for heart patients?

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for a complete teriyaki grilled mero set meal. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted component averaging using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for grilled sea bass, steamed white rice, teriyaki sauce, and miso soup. Chilean sea bass (mero) is approximated using USDA 'Fish, sea bass, mixed species, cooked, dry heat' as the closest approved-source equivalent. Input values (ai_generated, confidence 0.7) are consistent with USDA component estimates and are retained as the primary numeric basis. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using white rice as the dominant carbohydrate source.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite set meal is not available in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database as of 2024; mero (Chilean sea bass) is known to be rich in omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids but a precise per-100g composite figure cannot be derived from available approved sources without detailed recipe-level data. potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: micronutrient and moisture data for this composite dish are not available as a single entry in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database as of 2024. 2. Mero (Patagonian toothfish / Chilean sea bass) is not separately listed in USDA FoodData Central; USDA SR Legacy 'Fish, sea bass, mixed species, cooked, dry heat' (FDC ID 175159) is used as the closest approved-source equivalent and this substitution is documented. 3. GI of 64 is an analogous estimate based on white rice (University of Sydney GI Database); the composite meal GI may be modestly lower due to protein and fat co-ingestion. 4. Halal status is not confirmed; teriyaki sauce typically contains mirin (rice wine) which may not be halal-certified. 5. The dish may optionally include tempura as a side; this variant would increase energy, fat, and carbohydrate values above those reported here.
